entry.title: Re: Consume REST SCIM API of SAP IAS
entry.content:
Can you check the network trace?

The url of your ajax call needs to be added to the hostname + unique id of the app and not just after the hostname. In that case, it won't be resovled by the approuter.

entry.title: Re: Regarding Best Practices Client
entry.content:
Once the Best Practices have been activated and validated, your functional teams can proceed with creating transactional data. The Best Practices activation is designed to provide ready-to-use configurations for your processes without requiring further customization.

However, before moving forward with transactional data, it is important to:

  1. Confirm that the activated Best Practices fully meet your business requirements.
  2. Test the system in a controlled manner to ensure that everything functions as expected.

If you plan to activate additional Best Practices in the future, this should generally not affect the previously activated Best Practices. That said, it is recommended to thoroughly test any new Best Practice activation in a development or sandbox environment to confirm there are no configuration conflicts. Always validate the impact on transactional processes before rolling out any changes to production.

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition/assign-roles-to-your-site

entry.title: Turbocharging Machine Learning on SAP Data with Datasphere, FedML, and NVIDIA
entry.content:
Built on SAP BTP, SAP Datasphere enables integration and a unified view of semantically rich SAP data with third-party data across the enterprise landscape to help customers adapt faster to market changes and make more efficient and better-informed decisions.

With SAP Datasphere, data scientists can confidently access a high-quality data fabric using AI and machine learning (ML) models. To accelerate SAP’s federated machine learning (FedML) capabilities for SAP Datasphere, SAP is facilitating easier access to data for data scientists and enhancing ML workload performance with the support of NVIDIA accelerated computing platforms and NVIDIA AI Enterprise data science software such as NVIDIA RAPIDS.

FedML, a widely adopted Python library in enterprises, empowers data scientists to conduct comprehensive machine-learning experiments using federated SAP business data. This popular tool has now expanded its capabilities to support accelerated machine learning workloads by leveraging the power of NVIDIA accelerated computing, including the NVIDIA AI Enterprise software platform for generative AI, further solidifying its position as a go-to solution for mission-critical machine learning use cases in enterprises. 

As a leading AI platform, SAP DataSphere’s FedML enables organizations to collaborate on machine learning projects while keeping sensitive business data decentralized and secure. It offers a range of federated learning algorithms and supports various machine learning tasks, including classification, regression, and clustering. FedML's architecture is designed for scalability, allowing it to efficiently handle large-scale distributed computing environments.

One of FedML's key strengths is its seamless integration with SAP's ecosystem, making it particularly valuable for businesses leveraging SAP technologies. Its developer-friendly library and comprehensive documentation have contributed to widespread adoption among data scientists and machine learning practitioners across industries.

Accelerated Computing with NVIDIA: FedML leverages the NVIDIA RAPIDS suite of libraries,  including cuDF and cuML to enable accelerated data processing and model training on GPUs. This integration allows data scientists to access and load SAP business data directly from SAP Datasphere models into cuDF, streamlining the process for GPU-based training.

By enabling this direct data pipeline, FedML eliminates common challenges such as data duplication, error-prone ETL processes, and the need for intermediate data transformation or staging. This seamless workflow to ensure data integrity leverages  RAPIDS to enhance efficiency throughout the machine learning lifecycle, from data access to model training on high-performance NVIDIA GPUs.

FedML with NVIDIA GPU support

Getting started with FedML library in NVIDIA GPUs:

Step 1. Install the FedML library.

 

 

%pip install fedml-dsp

 

 

Import the required libraries

 

 

 

from fedml_dsp import DbConnection, Fedml 
import cudf, cuml, cupy 
import json

 

 

The presence of GPU is auto-detected to load RAPIDS CUDA libraries automatically and seamlessly. The library is ML-platform agnostic and hence can be used to take advantage of GPU processing in any ML platform that supports NVIDIA GPUs. 

Step 2. Connect to SAP Datasphere

 

 

with open('config.json', 'r') as f:   
config = json.load(f)   
db = DbConnection()

 

 

 

The connection to SAP Datasphere is securely established based on Python API based specification -  PEP 249 via TLS /SSH protocols.  SAP Datasphere’s unified data models are now accessible via the OpenSQL Schema and scope is limited to the space with the database user using which the connectivity was established. 

Step 3. Read data from semantic models of SAP Datasphere directly into CUDF

 

 

data = db.get_data_with_headers_cudf('SMALLCOVTYPE_VIEW', 1)

 

 

With just a single line, the data from SAP Datasphere models can be directly read into the cuDF for access with cuML & cuPy.  

Step 4. Pre-process the data  

 

 

 

def preprocess_data(concat_data):   
    #map categorical values to numbers   
    label = concat_data['cover_type']   
    #fix datatypes   
    df_X = concat_data.drop(['cover_type'], axis=1)   
    df_X = df_X.astype('float64')   
return df_X, label  
  
df_X, label = preprocess_data(data)  
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = cuml.train_test_split(df_X, lable, train_size=0.8)

 

We would then continue with the regular training steps:

Step 5. Train the logistic regression model using fit method.  

 

 

from cuml import LogisticRegression 
model = LogisticRegression().fit(x_train, y_train)

 

Step 6. Save the model

 

 

Import joblib
joblib.dump(model, 'LR_model.pkl')

 

 

Step 7. Deploy to AI Core & inference ...[optional]

FedML now supports deploying trained models directly onto SAP AI Core from the ML platform’s notebook. Refer to the below code on how to do the same: 

Step 7.1 Connect to SAP AI Core

 

 

fedml = Fedml(aic_service_key='aic_service_key.json')

 

Step 7.2 Onboard ai core resources

 

 

fedml.onboard_ai_core(create_resource_group=False, 
resource_group="", 
onboard_new_repo=True, 
github_info_path="github_info.json", 
secret_path="secret.json")

 

 

Step 7.3 Register application

 

 

application_details = { "application_name": "", 
"revision":"HEAD", 
"repository_url": "https://github.com/username/repo_name", 
"path": "deployment"}

fedml.register_application(application_details=application_details)

 

 

Step 7.4 Deploy the model

 

 

deployment_config = { "name": "", "resource_group": "", "scenario_id": "", "executable_id": "" }
endpoint = fedml.ai_core_deploy(deployment_config=deployment_config)

 

 

Step 7.5 Inference the endpoint

 

 

headers = {"Authorization":fedml.get_ai_core_token(), "ai-resource-group": "", "Content-Type": "text/csv"}

response_data = fedml.ai_core_inference(endpoint=endpoint,headers=headers,body=x_test.to_json(orient='records'))

 

 

Performance Metrics: GPU vs. CPU

To quantify the advantages of GPU acceleration, we conducted a comparative analysis between cuML algorithms running on GPUs and their CPU counterparts. Our benchmarks, performed across various data volumes and feature sets, revealed significant performance improvements. The following acceleration figures were documented:

RandomForest:  

Features \ Samples

1000

10,000

100,000

20

2x

10x

76x

50

2x

23x

116x

100

2x

35x

151x

200

3x

49x

190x

XGBoost Classifier:

Features \ Samples

1000

10,000

100,000

20

1x

7x

55x

50

1x

14x

91x

100

2x

19x

122x

200

3x

25x

148x

Logistic Regression Classifier:

  Features \ Samples

1000

10,000

100,000

20

14x

11x

136x

50

17x

22x

188x

100

35x

31x

205x

200

25x

28x

169x

 

Performance Gains: Our benchmarks revealed impressive acceleration rates, with GPU implementations outperforming their CPU counterparts by factors ranging from 10 to 200 times, depending on the specific data volume and feature complexity.

Real-world Applications: These experiments focused on tree-based models widely adopted across various industries. Such models are integral to solving diverse business challenges, including:

  • Inventory forecasting in supply chain management
  • Ad ranking in digital marketing
  • Medical diagnostics in healthcare

These use cases represent just a fraction of the real-world enterprise scenarios where GPU-accelerated machine learning can deliver substantial value and efficiency gains.

Final Thoughts:

This solution offers a powerful combination of advantages:

  1. Instant data access: By directly tapping into SAP Datasphere, we eliminate the need for time-consuming and costly ETL processes.
  2. GPU acceleration: Harnessing NVIDIA accelerated computing significantly speeds up model training.
  3. Semantic integrity: Working with data in its original context preserves valuable business semantics and relationships.

The combination of these factors results in a markedly lower total cost of ownership (TCO) compared to traditional CPU-based approaches that rely on extracted, transformed data stripped of its rich context. This integrated solution accelerates the machine learning workflow and enhances the quality and relevance of insights derived from SAP business data.

How to Try

FedML is pip installable ( https://pypi.org/project/fedml-dsp/  )  and can be tried with Trial SAP Datasphere instance .

Step by step instruction to try fedml is documented in the github : https://github.com/SAP-samples/datasphere-fedml/tree/main/Datasphere

FedML can also be tried for GPU model training on Hyperscaler ML Platforms. 

Credits

The above reference architecture and validations are the results of teamwork and contributions from both SAP and NVIDIA.  We would like to thank the NVIDIA team, and the following SAP team members specifically for their efforts: Jack Seeburger, Ran Bian, Karishma Kapur, Alex Bishka & Sandesh Shinde. And Sivakumar and Anirban Majumdar for their valuable support and guidance.

entry.title: Who Will Win? Witness the Ultimate Partner Innovation Showdown at SuccessConnect
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce a brand-new experience at SuccessConnect in Lisbon, Portugal, this year: the Partner Innovation Showdown! This live event brings together top SAP SuccessFactors partners to present their latest innovations in a friendly competition, with the chance for attendees to get involved, vote, and crown a winner. If you’re ready to be inspired by the cutting-edge solutions that are reshaping HR, this session is a must-see.

Connect, Engage, and Be Inspired

Taking place on Tuesday, October 29th, from 1:00 – 2:00 PM on the Show Floor in Theater 1, the Partner Innovation Showdown will be an exiting contest where innovation comes alive. With the venue located right next to the Partner Innovation Demo Pod and lunch area, you can easily grab a bite to eat and then settle in for an hour of groundbreaking ideas.

Five outstanding partners – Grayscale, Sodales, Beamery, TalenTeam, and Degreed – will each take the stage for a seven-minute pitch, followed by a brief Q&A. Attendees, including SAP judges and customers, will have the chance to vote live using Mentimeter, making this a truly interactive experience. By the end, we’ll reveal the most innovative solution and celebrate a winner!

Why Attend?

  • Witness Top Solutions: The Showdown will highlight real-world use cases that demonstrate the latest advancements in the SAP SuccessFactors suite of solutions.
  • Interact and Influence: As part of the audience, you’ll play an active role in determining which solution stands out, gaining insights and helping select the winner.
  • Get Inspired by Partner Innovation: Our SAP SuccessFactors Partner Ecosystem is a hub of ingenuity. See firsthand how these innovations can empower your business and inspire you to take your own HR strategy to the next level.

Add the Partner Innovation Showdown to your agenda builder today, and get ready to engage with the future of HR innovation at SuccessConnect. Don’t miss out on this exciting opportunity to see our partners in action!

entry.title: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System
entry.content:
Introduction

From 2402 Release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition added a new system monitoring capability, collectively called the Technical Monitoring Cockpit for monitoring the ABAP system.  The Cockpit provides a seamless connection between your system workload and single ABAP statistics records (main records and sub-records) down to SQL statements and their prepared plan.  Now you have a good starting point to conduct performance analysis on the SAP S/4HANA system in case of high response times, especially when you are an administrator or a developer. 

As this Technical Monitoring Cockpit is not unique to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and the Business Technology Platform, the discussion here applies to them as well.

I plan to tackle this topic in a series of blogs in bite sizes so that you can easily consume them during your day-to-day work.

  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through: this blog provides a guided tour of the Cockpit to readers, walkthrough (clicking through) various functions and resolve some confusions.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts: this blog explains what metrics we are looking for in the Cockpit, and how they can be helpful to you, especially when something seems suspicious. 
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload:  this blog discusses the approach as an administrator/developer to analyze the system performance daily.
  • Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Analyzing Performance with A Customized Profile (to be published):  this blog discusses how to take the advantage of setting up a profile to record system data you are interested, hence analyze the data when issues surface.

In my previous two blogs of this Technical Monitoring Cockpit series, I explained how to click through these apps and the key concepts, now let’s put them into use in your own system: how to do a system analysis as an administrator/developer daily.

 

System Workload

When talking about the system monitoring, the first entry is the System Workload app (see my previous blog on how to access it).

The below screen System Workload: Response Time Contribution gives a glimpse on how the system is utilized in the past 24 hours (Note: my most business users are in Asia, so the heavy workload appears in my midnight to early morning since I am located in US East Coast.)

  • During the past 24 hours (12 pm on Oct. 9 to 12 pm on Oct. 10), heavy workload happened around 2 am to 6 am.
  • The peak Response Time Contribution reached 360 seconds.
  • If I am curious on the peak Response Time Contribution, I can move the Time Slider to left. Comparing with two graphs of Oct. 8 vs. 9, we found
    • The peak Response Time Contribution is about the same, 300+ seconds, although they occurred at a different time slot.
    • When focusing on the highest Response Time Contribution, the pattern is similar with three high components: the Roll Wait Time, the ABAP Processing Time and the HANA Execution Time.

The Peak Response Time Contribution from 12 pm to 12 pm on Oct. 9

 

The Peak Response Time Contribution from 12 pm to 12 pm on Oct. 8

 

Now let’s narrow the time range to 2:30 – 3:15 am. I want to find out the reason of high Roll Wait Time, ABAP Processing Time and HANA Execution Time.  By clicking on Roll Wait Time in the Legend, we can see the Remote Function Call (RFC) is the main contributor, almost 93% ((56.36+56.24+49.39+8.99+8.97+8.27)/201.76). The top three programs are QDEST_RUN, QDEST_RUN_DESTINATION and ARFC_RUN_NOWAIT.

Explore the Cause of Long Roll Wait Time

 

The Roll Wait Time represents a time for which the roll context was rolled out. In the below figure, it is a major contributor to an RFC Process or waiting time until the results are returned from an external system.  This matches with the nature of an RFC call, i.e., you have no control over an external system on how fast it returns the result to you.

Contributors to the Server Response Time in a Synchronous RFC Processing (from SAP Help)

 

Now I click on the ABAP Processing Time in the Legend, the top ten contributors are listed in the table.  The top one is to run a program called /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources in an OData V2 type request.

Explore the Cause of Long ABAP Processing Time

 

Go to the details (right most > button of the record), we can see this program was run seven times as listed in the table.  The processing time is quite even, between 1.46 to 2.04 seconds.

ABAP Processing Time of Running Program /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources (At 10-Minute Intervals)

 

In the blog Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Key Concepts, I explained how the ABAP Statistics Data are collected: one minute at a time on those completed work processes (look back), and data are aggregated. How about we look at the data in a different granularity?  For example, select As Collected? That is a different view (see below).  However, we still cannot pin down which vertical purple bar belongs to which of these seven runs.

ABAP Processing Time of Running Program /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources (As Collected)

 

To get the details of each run, I click on the Details button at the right of the top row. This brings me to the ABAP Statistics Record screen with more information about this record.

In the tab Server Response Time Contribution, it provides all the metric data of this program. The large portion of the time is spent on the ABAP Processing and HANA Execution.

ABAP Statistics Record – Server Response Time Contribution

 

When I see the Database Network Time is 148.54 milliseconds, it tells me there are many trips to the database.  I want to explore further by clicking on the tab Data Connections. There are 26,326 database calls.  This confirms a lot of trips were made to the database.

ABAP Statistics Record – Database Connections

 

By clicking on the Details button again, we can see the Database Connection Details screen.  Although there are 26,326 logical database requests, only 333 physical database requests happened. From database point of view, it is efficient as most data are cached.

Database Connection Details

 

You can also explore the SQL statements associated with this long ABAP Processing Time by clicking on the tab SQL Statements.  When clicking on the first row of the program, you can see the details of this SQL statement.

SQL Statement Details

 

Sampled Work Process Data

In the blog Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Key Concepts, I discussed there are two perspectives of exploring system performance.  One is the System Workload, which has data at every minute on those completed work processes.  Another one is called Sampled Work Process Data.  It is looking at data at every second including those currently running work processes. I use this perspective to identify the running programs after I notice a peak resource consumption from System Workload.  Let me explain that.

The below figure displays the Sampled Work Process Data at the same time range as above, from 12 pm on Oct. 8 to 12 pm on Oct. 9.

Sampled Work Process Data (from 12 pm on Oct. 8 to 12 pm on Oct. 9)

 

It is tricky to isolate these colorful bars around 3 am.  If you try to narrow down to 2:30 – 3:15 am, you don’t see any data.  So, you have to enlarge the time range, for example 18:15 – 8:37. After applying filter of OData V2, I got the below chart and table.

Sampled Work Process Data: ABAP CPU Time (18:15 – 8:37)

 

In the previous section, I was investigating a high ABAP CPU Time with the following information:

  • Request Entry Type: OData V2
  • Request Entry Name: /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001
  • Program Context: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/DataSources
  • Start Time: 2024-10-09 02:34:57.92458200

 

Now I click on the first row with the same -  Request Entry Name: /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001.  A new screen opens with a time series of this Program Context but different Program Name. When I focus on the time range of 2:34:57.92458200, there are three programs running in that vicinity, namely:

  • /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP
  • CL_ACMRT_KERNEL_PROXY=========CP
  • /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP

Request Entry Point: OData V2 and /SAP/ESH_SEARCH_SRV/0001

 

Take the last program /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP as an example. I click on the Details button on the right. It reveals the Single Work Process Sample screen. I can review the Response Time and other information.

Single Work Process Sample – Work Process Metrics

 

When clicking the “ABAP Statistics Record” button, the ABAP Statistics for this Request Entry Point becomes available. Please keep in mind that this Record is an aggregated information of this Request Entry Point in the past minute, no longer at that second. But it is still good enough for you to isolate the program you are interested in.

ABAP Statistics Record for Program /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP

 

You can do the same for other two programs: /IWCOR/CL_DS_EP_HELPER========CP and CL_ACMRT_KERNEL_PROXY=========CP.

 

Conclusion

This blog discusses a daily routine work for an administrator or a developer on how to discover a high resource consumption activity, and find out the program causes that by using both the System Workload and Sampled Work Process apps.

 

References

  • SAP Help: Monitoring the System Workload
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Clicking Through
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – The Key Concepts 
  • Blog: Technical Monitoring Cockpit Introduction Series – Diving into Your Own System Workload
  • Blog: Analyzing performance degradations in the ABAP environment in the Cloud

entry.title: Re: Error consuming REST api in SAPUI5
entry.content:
The url of the app is missing in the api request. Normally, if you run your app in BTP it will be something like this: hostname + appid + index.html, eg: https://myapprouter.cf.hana.com/comacme.appbasicsalesorders/index.html

When you execute an API request, it should also contain the same path: hostname + appid + endpoint, eg:  https://myapprouter.cf.hana.com/comacme.appbasicsalesorders/jsonplacholder/yourapi

This is very well explained in this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-application-router/ba-p/13393550 

Section: Integration with HTML5 App Repo service

 

And also this one: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/fiori-application-programming-model-for-cloud-foundry-and-abap-cloud/ba-p/13388142

entry.title: Re: I am having trouble trying to create a program in SE38: /MERP/CORE_CPMS_APPCREAT_PROG.
entry.content:
Hi @Luis1001

It appears your system may be blocking HTTP connections. Could you please check if your system is configured to use a proxy server?

if your S/4HANA system is using a proxy or you are on a RISE system, you need to configure additional settings to allow the application creation process through /MERP/CORE_CPMS_APPCREATE_PROG. Can you check and confirm if you are having a RISE system or you are using a proxy ?

entry.title: Re: Custom SAP BTP Workzone Maintaince mode
entry.content:
The end date date could be set in your provisioning tool which will deactivate the user in IAS/CIS when it reaches that date.

You can create a plugin that reads the enddate from the provisioning tool or maybe from IAS/CIS and show a messages when it is getting closer to that date.

entry.title: Re: HOW to create a custom LOGIN page in CAPM in sap BTP
entry.content: You have to connect your BTP account with your own CIS where you can apply your custom branding as described here: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/use-custom-css

entry.title: webi time dimension returns blank in data; 1970 for 1/4/2024 when selecting year.
entry.content:
SYSTEM:
SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 4.3 Support Pack 4 Patch 2 Version : 14.3.4.4852.2
 
 
ISSUE:
A user updating a report to utilize a combined cube to replace merge dimension objects; has a "Date" object with multiple different values for each record returned.  For example: 1/4/2024.  When this date object shipDate is right clicked select time dimension, select YEAR is created and then added to the report; it is blank when placed next to the shipDate Object.  I can confirm the object is a "Date" data type.  When selected in it's own data block it displays 1/1/1970.  If the user users a formla to just display the YYYY portion, it works fine.  So why wouldn't time dimensions work?
 
THe restrictions identified here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESSOBJECTS_WEB_INTELLIGENCE/8ef44f11d1234d00bcd1505da525d961/166ae328546b4b8dacb1ee87b41820d4.html do not seem to apply:
  • Time dimensions aren't supported in SAP HANA Online mode.
  • Time dimensions don't support custom sorts.
  • Time dimensions don't support delegated measures.
  • Time dimensions don't support data tracking.
  • You can't use a report element that contains a time dimension as a shared element.
  • Time dimensions don't support translations for abbreviated Quarter, Semester and Week in data.
 
When I attempt this on a new report with just this date object; it works as expected returning 2024 or correct year for each shipDate Object.
 
My next steps are to slowly re-create this large report in a new report object to see if somehow the report became corrupt or determine if Combine Cube or Merge Dimension is somehow preventing the time dimension from being created properly.
 
QUESTIONs:
Has anyone encountered this situation before and if so were you able to resolve it? if so How?
Any other ideas on what could be cuasing this?

entry.title: Re: CORS Issue when Calling CAP Service from HTML5 Applic...
entry.content: Beside the solution from Wouter I think you should check out the examples at multi-cloud-html5-apps-samples. Either use the custom or managed approuter. Using xs-app.json you configure a proxy to the CAP backend and that way you can avoid CORS issues. Also the Approuter allows you to add authentication.

entry.title: Guided Experiences powered by SAP Build
entry.content:
Table of Content

  • Motivation for Guided Experiences

  • New Guided Process Modelling: Advanced Automation and Flexibility

  • Connecting Guided Processes as Wizard Templates in SAP Build Work Zone

  • Conclusion – SAP Build Guided Experiences

  • What's Next?

  • Additional Read

Motivation for Guided Experiences

Guided experiences are designed to simplify multi-step business processes by breaking them down into manageable and intuitive steps. Each step can be enriched with various types of content, including videos, documents, forms, tasks, UI cards, and apps to provide users with all the necessary information to complete each step. Steps can be classified as optional or mandatory, ensuring critical tasks are completed while allowing flexibility for other actions. Users don't need to worry about finishing all the steps at once as the progress is saved, enabling them to resume from where they left off. Guided experiences can be used for various complex processes such as parental leave, benefits enrolment, employee relocation, new hires onboarding or suppliers onboarding, training and enablement, and browsing and purchasing electronic equipment. To learn more about the background & motivation around guided experiences, please see our previous blog about this capability.

New Guided Process Modelling: Advanced Automation and Flexibility

The major enhancement in the October 2024 release is the ability to model guided processes directly in SAP Build Process Automation, bringing powerful process automation to your SAP Build projects. The following key aspects have been added:

Guided Process Artefact

The new ‘Guided Process’ artefact allows you to design and manage structured, multi-step processes within SAP Build Process Automation. This feature is perfect for creating more dynamic and reusable workflows.

Stages and Steps 

Following the structure introduced for the wizard layout, ‘Guided Processes’ equally allow to create stages & steps within each stage.  Additionally, these process steps can be designed to be sequential or parallel flows, allowing for both straightforward and complex workflows.

Create a StageAdd a Step to a Stage

 

Note: with this release, only SAP Build Work Zone is supported as ‘Step UI’; meaning the step itself needs to designed via the Work Zone wizard layout editor (for example to add UI integration cards).

Advanced Logic Integration

To create more sophisticated workflows, you can connect steps to conditions, subprocesses, and other business logic. This allows for advanced automation, ensuring processes respond dynamically to user actions or business rules.

Easy Deployment to SAP Build Work Zone

Once the guided process is modelled, it can be deployed to SAP Build Work Zone as a wizard layout template, ensuring easy availability for end-users. This provides a seamless transition from process design to execution.

Note: the deployment to SAP Build Work Zone is performed on behalf of the logged-in user via the automatically created destination pointing to DWS as per the following guide.

Shared Environments in SAP Build

This new ‘Guided Process’ artefact is only supported in shared environments, not in the default / public one. To learn more about shared environments in SAP Build, please refer to the following blog post.

Connecting Guided Processes as Wizard Templates in SAP Build Work Zone

In addition to modelling ‘wizard layouts’ directly in SAP Build Work Zone (which is now available via the “New Wizard” option)...

Add Wizard LayoutSelect Process for WizardOption for Standalone Wizard

 

… it is now possible to select ‘Guided Processes’ modelled in SAP Build Process Automation as the underlying structure when creating guided experiences.

Guided Process-based Wizards

Users can now select one of previously deployed guided processes they have access to. These serve as the template the wizard layout within SAP Build Work Zone.

Note: While modelling & deploying guided processes via the SAP Build Lobby requires access to the ‘advanced user’ license in SAP Build Process Automation, that is not the case for consuming deployed processes. For doing so, the user requires the following permissions:

  • Workpage edit permissions in Work Zone to edit the workpage, add wizard layouts,
  • ProcessAutomationParticipant role collection
  • 'Monitor' & 'Execute' permission for shared environment (Share an Environment | SAP Help Portal)

Note: creating wizard layouts based on ‘guided processes’ is currently released in preview environments of SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition / SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone and will become available in the non-preview environments with the subsequent release.

Auto-generated Wizard Structure

When selecting a guided process, the wizard layout is automatically generated based on the process definition, saving time and ensuring consistency.

Design Wizard with Widgets & UI Cards

Just like with a manually-created one, you can customize guided process-based wizard layouts using different widgets and UI cards, providing flexibility in designing the user experience.

  • Adding Widgets / Cards to each step: 
  • Example Step with Image widget:

Note: Already in a previous release, UI cards have been enhanced to interact with wizard layouts in SAP Build Work Zone, for instance to enforce users taking certain actions. Details and code examples for such UI cards are available via our central SAP Build Work Zone GitHub sample repository.

Process Instance Creation

When a user starts a wizard based on a guided process, an instance of that process is started automatically, allowing execution & resuming of the steps according to the defined process structure.

Conclusion – SAP Build Guided Experiences

This streamlined integration reduces development time and ensures that teams can leverage the power of SAP Build Process Automation alongside SAP Build Work Zone bringing exciting possibilities for creating powerful & content-rich guided experiences. These innovations not only simplify process design and execution but also ensure that you can deploy efficient, scalable guided experiences tailored to your unique needs – bringing different capabilities from SAP Build together via a deeply integrated, end-to-end experience!

What’s Next?

Looking ahead, we are planning to introduce additional features to further enhance guided experiences. These upcoming capabilities aim to add additional flexibility for creating, designing & operating guided experiences with SAP Build.  Here's a sneak peek at what's planned (*):

Spoiler
  • Repeating Guided Experiences
  • Starting Multiple Instances of the same Guided Experience
  • Support for more Step UIs, including Forms
  • Integration with Process Visibility for Monitoring
  • […]

In addition to those additional enhancements, we are also working on providing additional pre-built business content (packages) for guided experiences (*), such as onboarding in context of SAP SuccessFactors. Stay tuned as these new features roll out, offering even more ways to optimize your guided experiences and streamline your business processes!

(*) Disclaimer: Please note that the roadmap items shared here are subject to change and may not be delivered as initially planned. The information provided is for informational purposes only and does not constitute a commitment by SAP. As always, the best resource to learn about upcoming capabilities is the central roadmap explorer.

Additional Read

To learn more about the topic covered in this blog post, please also refer to the following assets & follow us on SAP community.

  • Related Blog Posts:
    • What’s new with SAP Build Work Zone guided experiences
    • SAP Build Process Automation - Introducing Environments
  • Learning Journeys & Certification: 
    • Work Zone: Implement and Administer SAP Build Work Zone | SAP Learning
    • Process Automation: Creating Processes and Automations with SAP Build Process Automation
  • Reference Architectures:
    • https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/refArchDetail/ref-arch-sap-build-work-zone
    • https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/refArchDetail/ref-arch-build-process-automation
  • SAP Help guides for more details:
    • Work Zone Preview: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-advanced-edition/sap-build-work-zone-advanced-edition/what... (will move to regular admin guide with next release)
    • Process Automation: Create a Guided Process
  • GitHub Repository for Work Zone Examples: https://github.com/SAP-samples/build-workzone-integration/tree/main/advanced/wizard

entry.title: Re: XML Transformation: Representation of internal table entries
entry.content:
For anyone having this same issue, creating a transformation with the following code did the trick for me:

<xsl:transform version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">

  <!-- Identity transform -->
  <xsl:template match=" @* | node()">
    <xsl:copy>
      <xsl:apply-templates select="@* | node()" />
    </xsl:copy>
  </xsl:template>

  <!-- Process any element that has "item" as child -->
  <xsl:template match="*[item]">
    <xsl:apply-templates/>
  </xsl:template>

  <!-- Replace "item" with its parent in source XML -->
  <xsl:template match="item">
    <xsl:element name="{name(..)}">
      <xsl:apply-templates/>
    </xsl:element>
  </xsl:template>

</xsl:transform>

@Arun_Venkatesh  - you can clear the shared memory by doing so 

TRY.
    CALL METHOD zcl_shr_areahandle_area=>free_instance
      EXPORTING
        inst_name = id_num    " Name of a Shared Object Instance of an Area it 
*       terminate_changer = abap_true                        " Writing processes will be ended
*       affect_server     = cl_shm_area=>affect_local_server " Servers on which the area is deleted or invalidated
      RECEIVING
        rc        = area_instance_rc.

    " Return Value (Constants in CL_SHM_AREA)

    CASE area_instance_rc .

      WHEN 0304192400.
        WRITE: 'Executed without errors'.
      WHEN 0304192401.
        WRITE:  'Operation fulfilled with execution'.
      WHEN 0304192402.
        WRITE	'Specified instance does not exist'.
    ENDCASE.
  CATCH cx_shm_parameter_error. " Incorrect parameter passed
    WRITE: 'error in clearing area instance'.
ENDTRY.

entry.title: Automated Case Overview report generation in Customer Insights dashboard
entry.content:
SAP4Me has launched a new analytical Customer Insights reporting feature that integrates data from various systems and dashboards, providing customers with a comprehensive overview of their SAP solutions in one place. This tool allows for tailored reporting to meet individual needs.

New Feature: Automated, Insightful, and Flexible Printable Snapshot Reports

We are excited to introduce a powerful new feature that enables the creation of automated, insightful, and flexible printable snapshot reports. This enhancement allows users to generate customized PDF reports with flexible filtering options, tailored to their specific needs. Users can select filters and timeframes to create focused and relevant reports.

Case Overview Report: A Comprehensive Analysis

The first report available, the Case Overview Report offers a detailed case situation insights and provides a comprehensive analysis of the key performance indicators essential to SAP support cases. The report highlights case volume and trends, the importance of maintaining business continuity, quick issue resolution, quality, and feedback.

 Customization and Authorization

Users can customize the Case Overview Report by adjusting filter settings and selecting the appropriate timeframe, enhancing its strategic value to their organization. The available filters are a subset of those in the Customer Insights dashboard, and authorization remains unchanged. S-users can run the report for a single account or the entire Customer Center of Expertise (CCOE).

Please note that S-users with installation-level authorization are currently not allowed to generate the report due to personal data security restrictions related to customer feedback data. Depending on user feedback, we may enable these S-users to run the report without feedback statistics.

When an S-user with account or CCOE-level authorization selects installations in the filter parameters, the feedback data remains unchanged and is displayed for all selected accounts.

 How to Generate the Report

To generate the report, click on the new "PDF Report Generator" button in the upper right corner. On the next screen, select the required report (currently, only the Case Overview Report is available, but more reports will follow). On the filter screen, select the required filters and timeframe. If no selections are made, the report will be generated for all authorized accounts for the default period of six months.

The PDF report will be generated automatically within a few seconds when you press "Download the Report."

 

 

 

 

 You will be informed when the download is completed and you can download and store the report locally if needed.

 

 

 

 

Report Contents

The report provides various key performance indicators (KPIs) around the cases sent and processed at SAP within the selected period. It also stores the selected filters, allowing you to understand the data later.

The report includes data on:

  • Case Volume: Number of cases sent to SAP by month, priority, channel, Solution Area, cloud/private cloud/on-premise, and the relation between open and closed cases.
  • Support and Customer Effort: Average processing time, Response Time ratio, and customer feedback on the support process.
  • Case Quality/Error Categories: Root causes of cases and the number of cases related to Customer/Partner issues.
  • Trend Comparison: Comparison of case volume, Response Time, Response Levels, Channels, Error Categories, and Customer Efforts with the same period in the past.

Benefits and Future Enhancements

The generated report provides a snapshot of the current situation, even as dashboard data is regularly updated. You can use these reports for internal reporting and collaboration with SAP support and the SAP front office.

We welcome your feedback and suggestions for further report automation and additional report types (e.g., system overview, orders and purchased products, or an overview across all available data for a particular product).

Please contact us to share your thoughts and help us improve this feature.

 

Please use the ‘Feedback’ slider on the right edge of the page of the application to share with us your feedback on this new tool and help us to improve the reporting functionality in SAP for Me.

Stay updated by subscribing to this blog and visiting the SAP Support Portal / Customer Insights.

SAP for Me is made for you!

Related blogs: SAP for Me goes reporting  or all related blogs to ‘Customer Insights’ reporting dashboard.

entry.title: Configure the remote web server to use HSTS
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We have received a security vulnerability from our security team and the fix was to Configure the remote web server to use HSTS. This has to be done on our sandbox database server. Could you please let us know the steps which can be taken to implement this on the database server.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Configure the remote web server to use HSTS
entry.content:
SAP HANA has two primary means by which it communicates with clients:

  • The HTTP Protocol
  • SQL Protocols (OBDC, JDBC, SQLDBC, etc.)

For the HTTP communication used for XS classic applications, SAP HANA implements an internal SAP Webdispatcher to route communication to the appropriate database for processing (see Maintain Standard HTTP Port Numbers with SAP HANA XS). Therefore, you can configure the webdispatcher to accomplish HSTS as per 3359291 - Configuring HSTS with Web Dispatcher or ICM.

The simplest means to disable non-TLS HTTP communication, is to reconfigure the webdispcher.ini > [profile] section from the SYSTEMDB by either:

  • Adjusting the icm/server_port_0 entry from PROT=HTTP,PORT=80$(SAPSYSTEM),PROCTIMEOUT=600 to PROT=HTTP,PORT=0,PROCTIMEOUT=600
    • As per icm/server_port_<xx> a port value of zero means that no inbound connections can be made for the specified protocol.
  • Removing icm/server_port_0 and instead specifying an icm/HTTP/redirect_<xx> to the HTTPS port.

For SQL communication, you can force TLS with configuration parameter sslenforce.

Best regards,
Tyler
SAP Technical Support | BTP HANA

お世話になっております。

Solution Managerのサポート廃止に伴い、Solution Managerの廃止を検討しています。

下記のNoteによると、Solution Managerを使用せずにリモートサービスの提供を受けられるとのことですが、Solution Manager自体を廃止(削除)しても、関連するSAPのシステムに影響はないのでしょうか？（Solution Managerを廃止し、Cloud ALMも利用しない形での運用を想定しています。）
なお、弊社システムではPERSON_NAMEレポートも利用しておらず、ここ数年のSolmanの利用はありません。

[Note]
2899458 - Remote Service Delivery without SAP Solution Manager


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Solution Managerの廃止(削除)について
entry.content:
ご担当者様

平素よりお世話になっております。

＞Solution Manager自体を廃止(削除)しても、関連するSAPのシステムに影響はないのでしょうか？

⇒関連するSAPのシステムというのは、Solution Managerにより管理対象システムのことに意味されていますか。

そうであれば、ご認識の通りで、Solution Manager自体を廃止(削除)しても管理対象システムに影響がありません。

以上、ご不明な点等ございましたらお知らせください。

宜しくお願いいたします。

SAPサポート

entry.title: Re: SAP IBP - Forecast consumption -Open Sales Order in t...
entry.content:
Forecast Consumption in Past bucket

Currently there’s no standard functionality to consume past dated forecast. This tweak of enhancement helps in past dated forecast consumption.

The example below shows the standard SAP system’s forecast consumption for First backward and then Forward consumption direction and 10 weeks backward and 10 weeks forward consumption.

Standard SAP Consumption: Sales Order of 14 Cases consume 6 from current week, 4 from next future week and 1 from next-to-next future week. But the consumption direction was first backward and then forward. But since standard SAP doesn’t allow consumption in past bucket that’s why all past dated forecast hasn’t been consumed at all.

(P.N.: Current week: TW41 2024)

 

 

 

Modifications to consume past forecast: Forecast Consumption performed for past dated horizon first

 

 

With 2 weeks backward consumption and 4 weeks forward consumption it consumes 2 calendar weeks (3 technical weeks) in the past and 1 calendar week or technical week in future (including current week)

 

 

 

Solution concept: Move the past dated forecast and sales order to future buckets by maximum backward consumption period allowed in your organization. This brings the forecast and sales order all in current-future bucket where SAP IBP easily allows consumption. Once consumed move back the forecast, sales order, forecast open output and total demand output back to the past bucket.

 

 

Solution Details: Create four stored keyfigures:

KeyFigure#1: ZCONSENSUSDEMANDQTY

 

KeyFigure#2: ZOPENORDERQTY2

 

KeyFigure#3: ZFORECASTOPENOUTPUT2

 

KeyFigure#4: ZTOTALDEMANDOUTPUT2

 

 

Make the keyfigures: FORECASTOPENOUTPUT and TOTALDEMANDOUTPUT as System Editable

 

 

Make following copy operators: Copy Operator#1:

 

Assumption: 10 weeks is the maximum backward consumption in your organization. Adjust this according to you need.

Copy Operator#2:

 

TS Forecast Consumption Profile:

 

Please adjust the forecast consumption level as Product/Location OR Product/Customer/Location as per the requirement

Create a single application template with following sequence to be maintained as steps:

Step 1: Copy Operator#1

Step 2: TS Forecast Consumption Profile

Step 3: Copy Operator#2

entry.title: about sums when expressions include ISNULL
entry.content:
Assuming that we have data as shown in the table below, when we embed an expression that outputs actual if actual exists, and outputs plan if it does not exist.
I want to output the data of ResultB as a total, but the result is the total of ResultA.
Is it possible to output the sum of ResultB?

           7     8     9     summary
actual 100 200        300
plan    300 300 400 1000

↓IF ISNULL(actual) then plan else actual
ResultA 100 200 400 300 ←not correct
ResultB 100 200 400 700 ←correct

entry.title: Issue with Starting SAP Fiori Development Space
entry.content:
こんにちは、

2024 年 9 月に Business Application Studio で SAP Fiori 開発スペースを作成しました。そのスペースで開発を行っており、数日前には問題なくこの開発スペースを起動できました。しかし、今日開発スペースを起動しようとすると、1 時間近く「開始中」のステータスで停止してしまいました。

その他の開発スペース (SAP Cloud Platform Mobile Services 開発スペース) は 1 分以内に起動します。

私の SAP Fiori 開発スペースに何が問題なのか、何か手がかりを持っている人はいませんか? また、これを「実行中」ステータスにする解決策を持っている人はいますか?

ワークスペースID: ws-wjjq4

さらにサポートや変更が必要な場合は、お気軽にお知らせください。

entry.title: Re: Cross Company Sales Return Process
entry.content:
Hi Vivek
You observation is correct. if you are running classical cross company returns (scope item 2F4), by default as system by design, the product received would be valuated - as in such cross company return scenario, by receiving the product in a different company, this indicates the ownership of product changes from customer to your company. therefore, returned product would be valuated. This is the by design.


At this moment, there is no direct work-around if you want to use the simple classical cross company returns. The potential way (for your reference) is that you receive the returned product firstly in the plant of sales company code (not valuated) with BKP, and they use follow up activity such as 0005 shipping to supplier (this treat your delivery company as a supplier). for your delivery company, then run the regular BKP customer returns process again, then you can valuate the product after material inspection. - This work-around might look complex than 2F4.

Best Regards,

Bin

entry.title: Looking for guidance to separate Datasphere workspace.
entry.content:
Hi team,

Hope you are well today. For those using SAP Datasphere or the older version productively, how do you logically separate your workspace? Do you separate it out by functional area e.g. MM, SD, FI or do you separate it out by business e.g. Finance, Supply Chain, HR etc or do you separate spaces based on Inbound Layer, Harmonization Layer, Reporting Layer or do you separate/create new spaces based on use case e.g. a use case to solve a data science problem and another use case to build operational reporting

Ideally, I think it should separate it out by functional area e.g MM, SD, FI and have a dedicated workspace just for master data which can be shared across the different workspace.

Would be interested to know what are people doing out there that is working for them.

Thank you for your time.

entry.title: Download CTMS transport logs by using Cloud Foundry CL
entry.content:
1. Background

little by little, the manual export/import has been replaced by the SAP BTP Cloud Transport Management Service(CTMS) for transporting any SAP Integraiton Suite(IS) Contents, including any Cloud Integration(CI) content and API management content.

When bellow error occurrs, how should we do to find out the cause? 

 The most straightforward way is to view the 'Transport Action Log' as below

obviously the information is not enough for you to determine the cause. You may notice the highlighted statement for details inform. In the below content, I will guide you reach there and see what kind of information those deployment log will provide and whether they are helpful or not.

1.1 Include

  • CF8 Installation
  • MultiApps CLI Plugin Installation
  • CF8 CL operation

1.2 Exclude

  • SAP CI Development
  • API Management Configuration
  • SAP BTP CTMS Configuration

1.3 Prerequsite

  • Multiple Tier(at least two) SAP Integration Suite Subaccount
  • CTMS has been configured between/among above subaccounts

1.4 Targer Reader

  • Integration Developer
  • Basis

Here are the detail steps:

2. Install CF CLI

This is the official link provided in the CTMS Log:

https://docs.cloudfoundry.org/cf-cli/install-go-cli.html

however i cannot find the one for windows platform. So if you are using Windows, please go to here:

https://github.com/cloudfoundry/cli/wiki/V8-CLI-Installation-Guide

Please ignore the 'Chocolatey' part. I have tried and find it tedious. You can download the installer file directly via here:

no need the admin access, you can install it and after installation, the folder looks like this:

3. Install MultiApps(MTA) CLI Plugin

This is the link provided in the CTMS Log for your reference:

https://github.com/cloudfoundry/multiapps-cli-plugin

My preference is this link:

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/install-multiapps-cli-plugin-in-cloud-foundry-environment

anyway the key part is here:

Please be aware that, in the example, the command cf has been used. whereas in our case, the CF8 is installed, therefore, cf8 is the relevant command for us. I won't explain it later. 

Launch the windows powershell, and go to the folder where cf8 has been installed so that you can execute it.

install the MTA plugin by executing below command:

 

 

cf8 add-plugin-repo CF-Community https://plugins.cloudfoundry.org

 

 

then

 

 

cf8 install-plugin multiapps -f

 

 

This is how it looks like on my machine. It might a little difference on yours. because the MTA addon has already installed on my computer.

4. Get into BTP Global Account via CF CLI

Find out the API Endpoint of the Cloud Foundry Space of the TARGET subaccount.

In my case, I have two BTP IS subaccounts. Say BTP IS Dev and BTP IS QA. Then go to the subaccount of BTP IS QA via web browser.

If you do not know how to find it, here is a reference of mine.

Now switch back to the windows powershell.

4.1 Specify the endpoint

This is the command format

 

 

cf8 api <your CF endpoint>

 

 

 This is an example of mine

The highlighted login command will be explained next.

4.2 Login Options

There are two ways to login, which has been highlighted in the above step.

by using cf8.exe login or simply cf8 login, you will login by using the default identity provider. 

If you have multiple identity provider, normally the Microsoft Active Directory, and you want to login by using any user under custom identity provider, please use cf8.exe login --sso or simply cf8 login --sso.

By this means, you will have the option to chose the identity provider and retrieve the login token.

For my case, I just login by using the default identity provider. So simply cf8 login

as you can see above, for me, there are four cloud foundry orgnizations under different subaccounts have been listed for me. For you, the list of orgs will be different.

please choose the ITEM NUMBER of the TARGET subaccount. 

So for my case, the target subaccount is 'integration-qa', so i should input '4' here. Please be aware that, DO NOT input item number of CTMS.

This is the operation for me.

5. Download Logs

Once you get into the subaccount or org, you can simple replicate the command from the CTMS Log and execute it here. Let's take the one in chapter 1 as an example

Now the relevant deployment log has been downloaded into your CF CLI folder as below. The folder name should be relevant to the deployment id used.

6. Analyse the log

The log folder created normally contains files like this

if the TR does contain multiple transported contents, there will be multiple log files with GUID format names in the folder.

This is an example of the content of the file whose name is with GUID format

This is an example of the content of the file, CTS_LOG

File OPERATION.log has most content. I just get part of them, which contains java exception captured.

Apparently these contents do not quite make sense to me as an Integration developer unfortunitely :-(. Not sure if they ring any bells to anyone who has basis background or to SAP.

7. My personal recommendation for BTP IS Transportation.

Here are few my experience when using CTMS v1.90.1 for transporting SAP BTP Integration Suite contents.

7.1 for Cloud Integration Content.

If the transport failed, you can manually delete the one in the target subaccount and import it again in CTMS.

7.2 For API Proxy of API Management

If the transprot failed, please try to manually export/import. If you encountered any error in the manul mode, it cannot be transported via CTMS. 

7.3 For product of API Management

My feeling is that, when a product has been transported in a TR, all of its API Proxy(s) will be transported at the same time. However when an API proxy is transported, the configuration in its product won't be transported. Therefore any transportation failure of any belonged API proxy will impact the transportation of product. 

If the transport of product failed, please check if all its API proxies are working fine and can be transported or not. 

By the way, I would recommend manully configure the product rather than transport it. Think about one of the API proxy is still under constructure by someone else and you transport the product. The TR will import the un-tested API proxy to the target which is not what you want. so, 

Transport on the API proxy level and manually add it to the product in the subsequential system 🙂 This is my personal proposal based on the current version of CTMS.

Conclusion

The downloaded deployment log looks not quite helpful. Further analysis is required on it. However it is a way to provide detail evidence to SAP for solving the transportation issue in CTMS.

entry.title: Custom Learning LxP Integration with SAP SuccessFactors LMS
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP SuccessFactors LMS offers Learning Experience Platform (LxP) integration via the Open Content Network (OCN). However, some clients may already be using a third-party LxP that they wish to integrate with SuccessFactors LMS. This document outlines the steps for such integration. Before proceeding, it is recommended to encourage clients to leverage the Open Content Network to simplify integration. If the client is not willing to transition, the following steps will guide the integration process.

Prerequisites:

  1. The LxP should support Single Sign-On (SSO).
  2. The LxP must be capable of sending data via API or flat file (SFTP).
  3. The dataset must include two types of tables:

   - Content Data: Includes content unique ID, content title, and content URL.

   - Learner Data: Includes content ID, user ID, date, etc.

Structure:

This document is divided into four parts:

  1. Login Method (SSO) for Accessing the LxP
  2. Sending User Data from SuccessFactors to the LxP
  3. Receiving Content Data from LxP into SuccessFactors
  4. Receiving Learning History from LxP to SuccessFactors

1. Login Method (SSO) for Accessing the LxP

Method 1: SuccessFactors Outbound SSO

You can connect the third-party LxP to SuccessFactors SSO similarly to other OCNs. Use SAML SSO (e.g., x256 encryption) to log in to the LxP.
- Advantage: Single login for users.
- Disadvantage: SuccessFactors only sends UserID and EmailID, so additional attributes need to be sent separately. Some LxPs may not support flat-file-based user creation.

Method 2: Identity Management Solutions (Recommended)

If your organization uses centralized identity management solutions like Azure AD, Okta, or ADFS, you can authenticate users for both SuccessFactors and the third-party LxP. The Identity Provider (IdP) should send the BizX UserID to both SuccessFactors and the LxP. Ensure that user IDs are identical between both systems to enable learner activity tracking.
- Advantages: Single login, standardized integration, no need for LMS-specific consultants.
- Disadvantage: If the IdP doesn’t provide the BizX UserID, integration may face challenges, but these can be mitigated in later steps.

Method 3: SAP Identity Management (IAS)

This method is similar to Method 2, where SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) manages user authentication across systems.

2. Sending User Data from SuccessFactors to the LxP

The LxP needs to create user profiles and send learner history back to LMS. Multiple options are available to send user data:

  1. Using Active Directory: Share available attributes from Active Directory to the LxP.
  2. Using Integration Center: Send user data via flat files.
  3. Using Post API: The LxP's API can receive user data directly.

Note: The LxP must receive the BizXID (UserID) from SuccessFactors. If this isn’t feasible, you can map email and user IDs via CPI during step 4.

3. Receiving Content Data (Item Data) from LxP to SuccessFactors

The only way to receive content data is through the item connector. This connector fetches content data from the LxP via API or flat file.

Item Connector Requirements:

- ItemID: Unique identifier for the content (required).

- Item Type: Optional, but helps distinguish content categories (can default).

- Item Title: Required.

- Creation Date: Optional (default values can be used).

- Content URL: Required.

- Other attributes, such as content description, are optional.

Best Practices:

- Keep common attributes (e.g., item type, revision date, revision ID) as defaults.

- If the content URL is static, only the item data connector is necessary.

- If the content URL is dynamic or multiple URLs exist for a single content item, you will need to create an additional connector file for item module data.

While the item connector can function with minimal data points, adding additional attributes can improve the overall integration.

4. Receiving Learning History Data from LxP to SuccessFactors

There are two methods for sending learning history data from the LxP to SuccessFactors LMS:

API Method

The preferred approach is to use the admin/learningevent-service/v1 API. This method allows real-time transmission of learning data from the LxP to SuccessFactors. CPI fetches the data from the LxP (via API or flat file), converts it to the LMS API standard, and sends it to LMS.

Learning History Connector

This alternative method involves the Learning History Connector. CPI collects learner history daily, sending it via SFTP on a scheduled basis. However, unlike the API method, this approach does not update records instantly.

 

 

By following these steps, you can successfully integrate a custom third-party LxP with SAP SuccessFactors LMS. This integration allows for seamless user access, content management, and learning history tracking across both platforms.

references:
2318341 - LMS Connectors Knowledge Support and Tips 
SAP SuccessFactors Learning OData APIs

entry.title: SAP Fiori Custom Variant Migration: A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
1. Prerequisites
    Exporting Custom Variants from the Source System

To migrate custom variants, you must first export them from the source system. Follow these steps:

Step 1: Open the SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • Log into your SAP Fiori Launchpad on the source system.
  • Navigate to the app where your custom variants are configured.

Step 2: Access the Custom Variant Management

  • Click on the filter icon or other settings icons where the variant settings are accessible.
  • Select the "Manage Variants" option.

     

Step 3: Export the Custom Variant

  • In the variant management dialog, locate the export option (this might be labeled differently based on the app or version).
  • Select the custom variant you want to export.
  • Click the "Export" button and save the file to your local system.

Note: The exported file will typically be in XML or JSON format, which will be used for importing on the target system.

  1. Importing Custom Variants to the Target System

Now that you have exported the custom variants, it's time to import them into the target system.

Step 1: Log into the Target System's Fiori Launchpad

  • Ensure you have access to the same app on the target system.
  • Open the Fiori Launchpad and navigate to the relevant app.

Step 2: Access the Import Functionality

  • Go to the variant management dialog, similar to the source system.
  • Locate the "Import" option.

Step 3: Upload the Exported File

  • Click the "Import" button and select the file you exported earlier.
  • Confirm the import, and the custom variant will be added to the target system.

Tip: Ensure there are no naming conflicts. If a variant with the same name already exists, consider renaming it before importing.

  1. Verify the Migration

Once the import is complete, verify that the custom variants have been correctly migrated. Follow these steps:

  • Access the app on the target system.
  • Check if the variants appear as expected.
  • Test the settings to confirm they match those from the source system.
  1. Troubleshooting Common Issues

Here are some common problems you might encounter during the migration process and how to resolve them:

Issue 1: Import/Export Button Not Visible

  • Ensure you have the correct authorization.
  • Check if variant management is enabled for that particular app.

Issue 2: Exported File Format Is Not Supported

  • Verify that both the source and target systems are compatible.
  • Check for SAP Notes or updates that address this issue.
  1. Conclusion

Migrating custom variants in SAP Fiori can save a lot of time and effort, especially when moving to a new environment or upgrading your systems. By following the steps outlined in this guide, you can ensure a smooth migration process.

Please comment on the issues not listed in this blog you have encountered during migration.
Cheers!
PS: Screen shots have been minimized in order to hide system data
